ingredients.name: tomato
ingredients.storage: Store at room temperature away from sunlight until ripe, then refrigerate. Refrigeration before ripening dulls flavor.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Tomatoes have a high water footprint; use overripe ones in sauces or soups rather than discarding.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.storage: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated place away from potatoes, which release moisture that speeds spoilage.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Onion skins and ends can be saved and frozen to make vegetable stock instead of being thrown away.

ingredients.name: cheese
ingredients.storage: Wrap in wax paper or parchment, then loosely in plastic, and store in the warmest part of the fridge (e.g. a drawer).
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Dairy has a notably high carbon footprint; freeze hard cheeses you won't use in time rather than discarding.

ingredients.name: bread
ingredients.storage: Store at room temperature in a bread box or paper bag; refrigeration speeds staling. Freeze for longer storage.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Stale bread can be turned into croutons, breadcrumbs, or bread pudding instead of being wasted.

ingredients.name: egg
ingredients.storage: Keep refrigerated in their original carton, pointed end down, away from the fridge door to limit temperature swings.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Eggs that are slightly past their best can still be tested with a float test in water before discarding.

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.storage: Keep refrigerated in its wrapper or a covered dish; can be frozen for up to 6 months.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Buy only what you need; butter has a high greenhouse gas footprint per kilogram relative to plant fats.

ingredients.name: rice
ingredients.storage: Store uncooked rice in an airtight container in a cool, dry pantry. Cooked rice should be refrigerated and used within 3-4 days.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Cooked leftover rice freezes well in portioned bags, reducing the chance of it going to waste.

ingredients.name: chicken
ingredients.storage: Keep raw chicken refrigerated below 4°C (40°F) and use within 1-2 days, or freeze immediately if not using soon.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Use bones and trimmings to make stock rather than discarding them, reducing both waste and the need for new purchases.

ingredients.name: spices
ingredients.storage: Store in airtight containers away from heat, light, and moisture to preserve potency.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Buy spices in small quantities or shared bulk amounts to avoid them losing potency before use.

ingredients.name: vegetables
ingredients.storage: Most fresh vegetables keep best refrigerated in a crisper drawer with adjusted humidity; leafy greens prefer high humidity, root vegetables lower.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Vegetable scraps (peels, stems, ends) can be frozen and used to make stock.

ingredients.name: pasta
ingredients.storage: Store dry pasta in a sealed container in a cool, dry pantry; cooked pasta should be refrigerated and used within 3-5 days.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Pasta water can be saved and used to thin sauces, reducing food and water waste.

ingredients.name: milk
ingredients.storage: Keep refrigerated at the back of the fridge, not the door, where temperatures fluctuate the most.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Milk nearing its date can be used in baking, sauces, or frozen in ice cube trays for small future portions.

ingredients.name: potato
ingredients.storage: Store in a cool, dark, well-ventilated place; avoid the fridge, which can affect taste and texture.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Potatoes are a high-yield, low-water-footprint crop; use sprouted eyes by cutting them out rather than discarding the whole potato.

ingredients.name: carrot
ingredients.storage: Remove leafy tops, then refrigerate in a perforated bag in the crisper drawer.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Carrot tops are edible and can be used in pesto or stock instead of being discarded.

ingredients.name: spinach
ingredients.storage: Store unwashed in a perforated bag in the crisper drawer; wash only right before use to avoid speeding decay.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Wilting spinach is still good cooked into soups, curries, or smoothies rather than thrown away.

ingredients.name: yogurt
ingredients.storage: Keep refrigerated and tightly sealed; avoid leaving at room temperature for extended periods.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Use yogurt nearing its date in marinades, baking, or smoothies to avoid waste.

ingredients.name: lemon
ingredients.storage: Store in the refrigerator crisper drawer for longest life, or at room temperature for up to a week.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Zest and juice can be frozen separately before the fruit spoils, extending its usable life.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.storage: Store whole bulbs in a cool, dry, well-ventilated place away from direct sunlight.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Sprouted garlic cloves are still usable; just remove the green sprout before cooking.

ingredients.name: mushroom
ingredients.storage: Store in a paper bag (not plastic) in the refrigerator to prevent moisture buildup and sliminess.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Mushroom stems, often discarded, can be used in stocks and broths for added flavor.

ingredients.name: bell pepper
ingredients.storage: Refrigerate in a perforated bag in the crisper drawer; keep dry to avoid mold.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Pepper seeds and cores can be composted rather than going to landfill with general waste.

ingredients.name: fish
ingredients.storage: Keep refrigerated at the coldest part of the fridge, ideally on ice, and use within 1-2 days or freeze immediately.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Choose sustainably sourced fish where possible, and use trimmings in stock to reduce waste.

ingredients.name: beans
ingredients.storage: Store dried beans in an airtight container in a cool, dry pantry; cooked beans should be refrigerated and used within 5 days.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Legumes have one of the lowest carbon and water footprints among protein sources, making them an eco-friendly substitute for meat.

ingredients.name: apple
ingredients.storage: Refrigerate in a perforated bag away from other produce; apples release ethylene gas that speeds ripening of nearby fruit.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Bruised or soft apples are ideal for baking, sauces, or smoothies rather than being discarded.

ingredients.name: banana
ingredients.storage: Store at room temperature; refrigerate once ripe to slow further ripening (skin will darken but flesh stays fine).
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Overripe bananas are ideal for baking (banana bread) or can be frozen for smoothies instead of being thrown out.

ingredients.name: yeast
ingredients.storage: Store unopened in a cool, dry pantry; once opened, refrigerate or freeze in an airtight container.
ingredients.sustainability_tip: Buying yeast in bulk and freezing portions reduces packaging waste over time.

recipe_templates.title: Cheesy Veg Toast
steps: Toast the bread slices until lightly golden.
Top with sliced onion and tomato.
Add a generous layer of cheese on top.
Grill or microwave until the cheese melts.
Serve hot.

recipe_templates.title: Simple Veg Rice Bowl
steps: Cook rice according to package instructions.
Sauté available vegetables with a little oil until tender.
Season with available spices.
Combine rice and vegetables in a bowl.
Serve hot, garnished with herbs if available.

recipe_templates.title: Garlic Tomato Pasta
steps: Boil pasta until al dente; reserve a splash of pasta water.
Sauté minced garlic in olive oil until fragrant.
Add chopped tomatoes and simmer into a light sauce.
Toss in the cooked pasta with reserved pasta water to loosen the sauce.
Season with salt, pepper, and herbs to taste.

recipe_templates.title: Quick Chicken Vegetable Stir-Fry
steps: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces and season lightly.
Heat oil in a wok or large pan over high heat.
Stir-fry chicken until cooked through, then set aside.
Stir-fry vegetables until just tender-crisp.
Combine chicken and vegetables, season with available spices, and serve hot.

recipe_templates.title: Basic Fresh Veg Salad
steps: Wash and chop all available vegetables.
Combine in a large bowl.
Add a squeeze of lemon, salt, and a drizzle of oil.
Toss well and serve fresh.

recipe_templates.title: Buttered Egg Toast
steps: Toast the bread and spread butter while warm.
Fry or scramble the eggs to your preference.
Place the eggs on top of the buttered toast.
Season with salt and pepper, and serve.

recipe_templates.title: Hearty Bean and Rice Bowl
steps: Cook rice according to package instructions.
Heat cooked or canned beans with available spices.
Combine beans over a bed of rice.
Top with any fresh vegetables on hand and serve.
